title: DraftKings | Company Profile, Sports Betting & Daily Fantasy 2026
subtitle: From a Boston startup to a $5 billion publicly traded sports betting and daily fantasy platform operating across 25 states
content_html:
What DraftKings Is

DraftKings (Nasdaq: DKNG) is one of the largest online sports betting and daily fantasy sports operators in the United States. Founded in Boston in 2012, the company has grown from a weekly fantasy football niche product into a publicly traded digital gaming platform with a presence in 25 states and estimated 2025 revenue of $4.77 billion.

DraftKings operates across three primary lines: an online sportsbook that accepts wagers on professional and college sports, a daily fantasy sports (DFS) platform where players build lineups and compete for prize pools, and an online casino product available in five states. The company went public in 2020 through a SPAC merger at a valuation of $3.3 billion and has since become one of the defining stocks of the sports betting era. For the broader regulatory picture in Texas, see How DraftKings and PrizePicks are legal in Texas.

Company History | 2012 to 2026

DraftKings was co-founded by Jason Robins, Matt Kalish, and Paul Liberman out of an office in Boston. The original product was a daily fantasy sports platform targeting the gap between season-long fantasy leagues and single-event sports betting. The company grew rapidly through aggressive marketing, signing media partnerships with ESPN, Turner Sports, and eventually the major professional leagues.

The 2018 Supreme Court ruling in Murphy v. NCAA struck down the federal ban on sports betting, opening the door for state-by-state legalization. DraftKings pivoted aggressively into online sportsbook operations, launching in New Jersey in 2018 and expanding to a new state roughly every two to three months for the next four years. The company also acquired Golden Nugget Online Gaming in 2022 to extend its casino footprint.

By 2025, DraftKings had reached profitability for the first time on an adjusted EBITDA basis, a milestone the company had targeted since its IPO. The path there required absorbing billions in customer acquisition costs during the early legalization land-grab.

Products | Sportsbook, Daily Fantasy, and Casino

The DraftKings Sportsbook is the company's largest revenue contributor. It supports single-game wagers, parlays, same-game parlays, futures, and live in-play betting across the NFL, NBA, MLB, NHL, college football, soccer, and more than 30 additional sports. The mobile app operates in every state where online sports betting is legal.

The Daily Fantasy Sports product, DraftKings' original business, lets users draft player lineups within a salary cap and compete for real-money prize pools. Contests range from head-to-head matchups to large guaranteed-prize tournaments. DFS operates under a different legal framework than traditional sports betting, which is why it remains available in states where sportsbook wagering is not yet legal, including Texas. For a direct comparison with a key DFS competitor, see the PrizePicks profile.

Revenue and Financials | 2025 Snapshot

Revenue growth has been consistent at 20-30 percent year-over-year since 2021, driven by new state launches and increasing handle per user. The company expects to continue expanding margins as customer acquisition costs normalize and early-legalization states mature.

Legal Footprint | US State-by-State Status

DraftKings Sportsbook operates in states that have passed sports betting legislation: New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Illinois, Michigan, New York, Colorado, Indiana, Iowa, Tennessee, Virginia, West Virginia, Arizona, Connecticut, Kansas, Louisiana, Maryland, Massachusetts, Ohio, Vermont, and others. Each state carries distinct tax rates, handle fees, and promotional restrictions that directly affect the company's per-state margins.

Texas, Florida, and California remain the three largest unlegalized markets. DraftKings has publicly lobbied for Texas legalization and maintains active DFS operations in the state through the separate regulatory classification of daily fantasy as a game of skill. The legal analysis underpinning Texas DFS operations is covered in depth at DraftKings and PrizePicks: Texas legality explained.

Competitors | FanDuel, BetMGM, ESPN Bet

DraftKings' primary sportsbook competitor is FanDuel, a subsidiary of Flutter Entertainment, which holds approximately 35-40 percent of the US online sports betting market by handle. DraftKings holds roughly 30 percent. BetMGM, ESPN Bet (Penn Entertainment), and Caesars Sportsbook split most of the remaining share.

In daily fantasy specifically, DraftKings competes with PrizePicks, Yahoo Fantasy, and Underdog Fantasy. The DFS segment is a smaller but strategically important wedge into sports markets where full sportsbook legalization has not yet occurred. Written by Max DeLeonardis, OzoneNews Finance Desk.
